content.text: Agent system cards are the primary transparency artefacts for agentic AI deployments: structured documents that describe what an agent does, what it can access, how it performs, and what its known limitations are. They extend the model card concept pioneered by Gebru et al. (2018) to address the additional complexity of autonomous agents that hold tool access, maintain state across interactions, and can take consequential actions in the world.

attrs.alt: Agent System Card structure showing nine sections: identity, intended use, model details, tool access, known limitations, performance metrics, human oversight, bias assessment, and governance

content.text: What it is

An agent system card is a structured documentation artefact that provides standardised, comprehensive disclosure of an AI agent's identity, intended purpose, capabilities, tool access, known limitations, performance characteristics, oversight mechanisms, and governance. It is the agentic-era extension of the model card concept, adapted to address the additional disclosure requirements that arise when a language model is embedded in an autonomous system that can execute tools, maintain context across sessions, and take actions with real-world consequences.

Unlike a model card for a static language model, an agent system card must document not just what the underlying model can do, but what the overall agent system is configured to do: which tools it has access to, what permissions those tools grant (read-only vs. write access, access to financial systems, ability to send communications), how the agent's behaviour is shaped by its system prompt and operator configuration, and what human oversight mechanisms are in place to review or reverse its actions. This additional layer of system-level documentation is essential because two agents built on the same base model can have radically different risk profiles depending on their tool access and configuration.
Why it matters

Agent system cards serve four distinct functions. For users and affected parties, they provide the information needed to make informed decisions about whether to interact with or rely on the agent's outputs. For compliance and legal teams, they constitute the evidence artefact that demonstrates the organisation has documented its agent's capabilities and limitations before deployment — a requirement under the EU AI Act's Article 13 transparency obligations and implicitly required by NIST AI RMF's GOVERN and MAP functions. For engineering teams, the discipline of authoring a system card surfaces assumptions and undocumented decisions that should be explicit. And for regulators and auditors, system cards are the starting point for any assessment of an agent's compliance with applicable requirements.

The failure to maintain accurate system cards has specific downstream consequences. When an incident occurs and regulators investigate, the absence of a current, accurate system card signals inadequate pre-deployment due diligence. If the card exists but is outdated — describing an earlier version of the agent that lacked the tool access that caused the incident — it is potentially misleading. System cards that accurately describe the agent at the time of harm, however, demonstrate that the organisation understood its system and had documented its risk profile, which is a significant mitigating factor in enforcement.
Benefits
Structured transparency
— Provides stakeholders with the same information about the agent, in a consistent format, regardless of who is asking — user, regulator, auditor, or internal reviewer.
Pre-deployment risk identification
— The act of writing a system card forces the team to articulate exactly what the agent can do and what its limitations are, which often reveals undocumented risks before they manifest in production.
Regulatory evidence
— A version-controlled history of system cards provides documentary evidence that the organisation assessed the agent's capabilities and limitations at each deployment stage.
Incident investigation support
— During an incident, a current system card provides a baseline description of the agent's intended behaviour, making it easier to identify where actual behaviour deviated from intent.
Cross-team communication
— System cards give product, legal, compliance, and support teams a shared reference document that describes the agent without requiring direct access to the engineering team.
Trade-offs and limitations
Static document for dynamic systems

— Agents change frequently: models are updated, tools are added or removed, system prompts are revised. A system card that is not updated in sync with these changes becomes misleading faster than traditional software documentation.
Capability disclosure tension

— Accurate disclosure of an agent's tool access and API integrations may reveal proprietary system architecture. The level of detail appropriate for a public-facing card differs from what is appropriate for internal governance use.
Emergent behaviour is hard to characterise

— Agentic systems sometimes behave in ways that were not anticipated during evaluation. Honest acknowledgement of the limits of characterisation ('this agent has been evaluated in scenarios X, Y, Z; its behaviour in other scenarios is uncertain') is more accurate but less reassuring than overconfident capability statements.
Maintenance burden
— In a fast-moving deployment environment, keeping system cards accurate requires a disciplined update process tied to the change management workflow. Without this, cards become stale within weeks.
How it works

An agent system card is authored at the time of initial deployment and updated whenever a material change occurs to the agent. The card is structured around a defined template with required and optional sections. At minimum, the card covers: agent identity (name, version, deployment date, responsible team); intended use (the tasks the agent is designed to perform, the intended user population, and explicitly out-of-scope uses); model details (the underlying model, version, any fine-tuning, and inference parameters); tool access (each tool available to the agent, what permissions it grants, and what external systems it connects to); known limitations (failure modes identified during evaluation, domain coverage gaps, language or cultural limitations); performance metrics (accuracy on representative tasks, latency characteristics, fairness metrics across relevant demographic groups); human oversight mechanisms (what triggers human review, how operators can inspect and override agent decisions, and what the escalation path is for high-stakes situations); and governance information (card owner, last review date, next scheduled review, regulatory classification, and how concerns about the agent can be reported).

For the performance section, the card should distinguish between what was evaluated in pre-deployment testing and what is observed in production monitoring. Pre-deployment metrics reflect controlled evaluation conditions; production metrics reflect the full distribution of real-world inputs, which will always include cases that fall outside the evaluation distribution. Cards that conflate the two overstate confidence in the agent's production behaviour.
Architecture

System cards are authored in a card management system — this can be as simple as a version-controlled Markdown file in the agent's repository, or as sophisticated as a purpose-built system card platform integrated with the model registry and deployment pipeline. The card is linked to the agent's deployment record so that the current version of the card is always accessible alongside the deployed agent version. Change events in the deployment pipeline (new model version, tool integration change, system prompt update) trigger card review notifications to the card owner. A card template library maintained by the AI governance team ensures consistency across all agents in the portfolio.
When to use it

An agent system card is required for every AI agent deployed in a production environment. For low-risk internal tools with a small user population, a lightweight card covering the essential sections may be sufficient. For high-risk agents that process sensitive data, take consequential actions, or operate in regulated domains, the full card with all sections is required, and the card must be reviewed by legal and compliance before deployment. For agents subject to EU AI Act high-risk classification, the system card content aligns with and supplements the required technical documentation under Article 11 of the Act.
How to implement

Define the organisation's standard system card template with required and optional sections, adapted from existing model card standards (e.g., Hugging Face model card format, Google Model Cards) with additional sections covering agentic-specific concerns.
Integrate system card creation into the pre-deployment checklist: no agent goes to production without a completed and reviewed card.
Store cards in version control alongside the agent codebase, linked to deployment records via the model registry. Each card version should reference the specific agent version it describes.

Define card update triggers in the change management process: model version change, tool addition or removal, significant system prompt revision, or meaningful change in performance metrics all require a card update before redeployment.

Establish two versions of each card: an internal version with full technical detail for engineering and compliance use, and an external version with appropriate redaction for public or customer-facing use.

Review all cards annually at minimum; high-risk agent cards should be reviewed quarterly. Build this into the periodic compliance attestation cycle so that card currency is attested as part of the programme's compliance status.
Examples

# agent-system-card-v2.3.yaml
# Customer Service Agent - System Card
# Last Updated: 2025-03-15 | Next Review: 2025-06-15

identity:
  name: CustomerAssist
  version: '2.3.0'
  deployment_date: '2025-01-10'
  responsible_team: AI Products
  card_owner: ai-governance@company.com
  card_version: '2025-03-15'

intended_use:
  primary_tasks:
    - Answer product questions using knowledge base
    - Process return and exchange requests
    - Escalate billing disputes to human agents
  intended_users:
    - Authenticated retail customers
    - Customer service supervisors (monitoring mode)
  out_of_scope:
    - Legal advice or regulatory guidance
    - Medical or health information
    - Decisions affecting credit or employment
  geographic_deployment: US, Canada, UK

model_details:
  base_model: claude-sonnet-4-5
  provider: Anthropic
  fine_tuning: none
  context_window: 200000 tokens
  inference_params:
    temperature: 0.3
    max_tokens: 1000
  system_prompt_version: 'SP-2025-03-A'

tool_access:
  tools:
    - name: knowledge_base_search
      permissions: read-only
      external_system: Elasticsearch cluster (internal)
      data_access: Product documentation, FAQs
    - name: order_management
      permissions: read-write
      external_system: Order management API
      data_access: Customer order history, return initiation
      write_actions: [initiate_return, apply_discount_code]
    - name: escalation
      permissions: write
      external_system: CRM ticketing
      write_actions: [create_support_ticket, flag_for_supervisor]
  tool_call_logging: all calls logged, 24-month retention

known_limitations:
  - Agent may misidentify product variants with similar names; human review recommended for high-value returns
  - Knowledge base accuracy degrades for products released within 30 days of last index refresh
  - Performance in French Canadian (fr-CA) is materially lower than English; recommend English-only deployment in production until evaluated
  - Agent does not have access to real-time inventory; availability statements may be inaccurate

performance_metrics:
  evaluation_period: 'Q1 2025'
  intent_classification_accuracy: 0.94
  return_process_completion_rate: 0.91
  escalation_precision: 0.88
  latency_p50_ms: 1200
  latency_p99_ms: 4800
  fairness:
    demographic_parity_score: 0.97
    evaluation_groups: [age_18-34, age_35-54, age_55+]
    fairness_evaluation_date: '2025-02-28'

human_oversight:
  review_triggers:
    - Customer explicitly requests human agent
    - Order value exceeds 00
    - Three or more failed resolution attempts
    - Escalation tool is invoked
  override_mechanism: Supervisor can view full session transcript and take over
  audit_sampling: 2% of sessions reviewed weekly by QA team

governance:
  regulatory_classification: 'Low risk (internal classification)'
  eu_ai_act_classification: 'Not high-risk (Annex III review: N/A)'
  data_protection_review: 'DPIA completed 2024-12-01'
  incident_reporting_contact: security@company.com
  user_concern_channel: 'In-product feedback; privacy@company.com'
Real-world example

A large insurance company deploying an AI agent to assist underwriters with risk assessment authored a system card that disclosed the agent's access to public property records APIs, internal claims databases, and credit reporting integrations. In the limitations section, the card honestly stated that the agent's performance on commercial property risks was evaluated on US properties only, and that its outputs for international properties had not been validated. The card also disclosed that the agent's risk scoring showed a 6% disparity between properties in majority-minority postal codes versus other areas, and that this disparity was under active investigation.

When the state insurance commissioner requested information about the company's AI underwriting tools during a routine market conduct examination, the company provided the system card — both the internal version and a redacted external version. The examiner credited the company's proactive disclosure of the demographic disparity, noting that the company had identified the issue itself and was actively investigating it. This stood in contrast to other carriers in the examination who had no equivalent documentation, who received findings requiring them to retroactively characterise their AI systems.
Common mistakes
Treating system cards as marketing documents
— System cards that emphasise capabilities and omit or minimise limitations are counterproductive for governance purposes and may mislead regulators and users.
Single version for all audiences

— A card containing full technical detail (including system prompt structure or API integration specifics) should not be published publicly; but a card with excessive redaction is useless for governance. Maintain internal and external versions.
No update process
— System cards authored at initial deployment and never updated become misleading within weeks as the agent evolves. Card updates must be a required step in the change management process.
Omitting tool access detail

— A system card for an agent that can write to databases, send emails, or execute financial transactions must document these capabilities explicitly. Omitting consequential tool access from a card is a material omission.
Mixing base model limitations with agent system limitations

— The system card should clearly distinguish between limitations that arise from the underlying model (e.g., knowledge cutoff, language support) and limitations that arise from the system's specific configuration and tool access.
Best practices
Author the card before deployment, not after
— The discipline of completing a card before going live surfaces undocumented assumptions and design decisions that should be explicit.
Be specific about limitations

— Vague limitation statements ('may produce inaccurate outputs') are less useful than specific ones ('performance drops by approximately 15% on queries involving technical jargon outside the training domain').
Version-control all cards
— Maintain a complete history of card versions linked to the agent versions they describe. This history is essential for post-incident investigation.
Assign card ownership to a named individual
— Cards without owners are not updated. Each card should have a named owner with review scheduled in their workplan.
Integrate card review into the attestation cycle
— Attestation of card currency and accuracy should be a required element of periodic compliance attestations for each AI system.
Performance and scalability

Maintaining a portfolio of system cards scales in proportion to the number of agents deployed and the rate of change in each agent. For organisations with ten or fewer agents that change infrequently, a simple template-based approach in a version-controlled document store is sufficient. For organisations with dozens of agents updated frequently, automated card generation from structured metadata in the model registry (base model, tool list, deployment parameters) can reduce the manual authoring burden, with human review focused on the judgment-intensive sections (limitations, oversight mechanisms, fairness assessment).

Tool
Category
Role
Hugging Face Model Card format
Standard template
Widely adopted base format; extend with agentic sections
MLflow Model Registry
ML lifecycle
Store card alongside model version; link card to deployment record
OneTrust AI Governance
AI GRC
Structured card authoring, review workflows, and portfolio management
Confluence / Notion
Documentation
Lightweight card storage for teams without dedicated GRC platforms
Comparison with alternatives
Artefact
Strengths
Limitations
Agent system card (this topic)
Comprehensive, structured, standardised; covers full agent system
Requires maintenance discipline; risks becoming stale
Traditional model card
Well-established format; widely understood
Does not cover tool access, system prompt, or oversight mechanisms
Technical documentation (README)
Familiar format; developer-friendly
No standardised structure; not accessible to non-technical stakeholders
Impact assessment report
Deeper analysis of specific risk areas
Point-in-time; not designed for ongoing reference; less structured
Security and reliability

Internal system cards contain sensitive information including the agent's system prompt structure, tool API credentials references, and known vulnerability details. These cards must be stored with access controls that restrict access to the engineering team, compliance, and legal counsel. External system cards published to users or regulators must be reviewed for inadvertent disclosure of security-sensitive details before publication.

Card version history must be maintained even when cards are updated, because the historical version of the card at the time of any incident or complaint is legally relevant. Do not overwrite historical card versions; archive them with clear version identifiers and dates.
Key takeaways

Agent system cards extend model cards to address the additional disclosure requirements of autonomous systems: tool access, system prompt configuration, oversight mechanisms, and agentic failure modes.
Cards must be maintained in sync with agent changes; an outdated card is potentially misleading and fails its purpose as a governance artefact.

Maintain both internal (full detail) and external (appropriately redacted) versions of each card; the level of disclosure appropriate for public use differs from what is needed for internal governance.
Honest disclosure of limitations and known issues — including demographic performance disparities — is treated more favourably by regulators than omission, which signals inadequate self-assessment.
Version control of card history is essential; the card version at the time of an incident is legally relevant evidence.
Integrate card authoring into the pre-deployment checklist and card updates into the change management process; governance requirements without process integration are not reliably met.
Card currency should be attested as part of the periodic compliance attestation cycle, creating a formal accountability link between documentation accuracy and governance programme compliance.
